title: Practice Questions Lecture 3 With Answers
body:
A 30-year zero-coupon bond with face value of $1,000 has an annualized yield to maturity
of 6.4%. Its market value is:

             a.        $151.08
             b.        $155.51
             c.        $643.06
             d.        $661.88
             e.        None of the above




A bond with 10% coupon paid semi-annually, 1 year maturity, and $1,000 face value has an 8%
annualized yield to maturity. Its market value is:

             a.        $1,009.62
             b.        $1,018.52
             c.        $1,018.86
             d.        $1,035.67
             e.        None of the above.




Which of the following is true?

             a.       An investor is sure to earn a total holding period return on the bond equal
             to the bond’s YTM if she holds it until maturity and if the issuer does not default.
             b.       In equilibrium, two plain vanilla bonds from the same issuer should have
             the same YTM even if they have different coupon rates and maturities.
             c.       When computing the total holding period return on a bond investment
             strategy we must assume that the coupons are reinvested at the YTM.
             d. The 2-year spot rate is the YTM of the 2-year zero coupon bond.




Which of the following bonds is the least sensitive to interest rate changes?

             a.        15 years maturity, 5% coupon.
             b.        15 years maturity, 3% coupon.
             c.        9 years maturity, 3% coupon.
             d.        9 years maturity, 5% coupon.
               e.       9 years maturity, Zero-coupon.




For a plain vanilla bond, the convexity is .

              a.       positive, and higher when interest rates are lower.
              b.       positive, and higher when interest rates are higher.
              c.       negative, and decreases as interest rates increase.
              d.       negative, and increases as interest rates increase.
              e.       none of the above.




The yield curve is flat. You think                    that   rates    are     going   to   increase
soon. What is your best investment strategy?

              a.       Buy a 20-year bond.
              b.       Buy a 1-year bond.
              c.       Sell a 20-year bond.
              d.       Sell a 1 year bond.



A bond that pays semi-annual coupons with an annualized YTM of 6% (3% semi-annual) trades
at $1,250. Its duration (not modified) is 14.42 semesters. If the YTM increases to 6.16 %, the bond
price will decrease by about:

              a.       $6
              b.       $10
              c.       $14
              d.       $18
              e.       $22




To immunize/hedge a bond portfolio against interest rate movements, you could:

              a.       sell a coupon bond with same maturity as the portfolio.
              b.       sell a bond with same duration and YTM as the portfolio.
              c.       sell a zero-coupon bond with maturity equal to the modified duration of the
              portfolio.
              d.       purchase a bond with the same duration of the portfolio.
 Duration tends to …..

            a.          underestimate price decreases, and overestimate price increases.
            b.          underestimate price decreases, and underestimate price increases.
            c.          overestimate price decreases, and overestimate price increases.
            d.          overestimate price decreases, and underestimate price increases.
            e.          none of the above.




A balance sheet is always properly immunized when ….

            a.          the duration of assets is equal to the duration of liabilities.
            b.          the modified durations of assets, and liabilities are equal.
            c.          the values of assets, and liabilities are equal.
            d.          none of the above.




For an investor, what is the benefit of buying a bond with duration equal to your investment
horizon (H)?

            a.      Take advantage of the reinvestment effect.
            b.      Take advantage of the price effect.
            c.      Have a bond which price is not sensitive to changes in interest rates.
            d.      To lock into a future value of your bond investment at your horizon H for
            reasonable movements of interest rates.




Which defines best the expected return on a coupon bond?

            a.          The face value divided by the bond price minus 1.
            b.          The YTM if the bond is sold before maturity.
            c.          The YTM if the bond is sold at maturity.
            d.          The coupon rate.
 A bond with 3 year maturity, 6% coupon paid semi-annually, trades at $974. Its modified duration
is 2.70 year, its convexity is 8.75 (in years). What is your best estimate of the price impact of a
200 basis points decrease in annualized interest rates?

               a.         $108.72 increase
               b.         $54.30 increase
               c.         $108.72 decrease
               d.         $54.30 decrease
               e.         None of the above.




An investor buys a bond with a maturity of 20 years, annual coupons of 14% and a face value of $100. He
sells the bond back after 3 years. If the coupons are reinvested at 11.5% during the holding period, the dollar
interests earned on the coupons is approximately:

                     a.   $5.02
                     b.   $10.42
                     c.   $47.02
                     d.   $42.00




An investor wishes to take advantage from her view that interest rates will decrease during the next months.
What bond should she buy to benefit from the largest possible increase in price?

                Maturity & Coupon rate
                                            a.     2032 & 10%
                                            b.     2032 & 12%
                                            c.     2047 & 10%
                                            d.     2047 & 12%




The yield curve is currently upward sloping. A bond portfolio manager anticipates that the yield curve will
flatten in the near future. What strategy should she use in order to take advantage from this movement?

                     a.   Sell short term bonds and buy long term bonds
                     b.   Sell short term bonds and buy short term bonds
                     c.   Buy mid-term maturity bonds
                     d.   Sell mid-term maturity bonds
 You consider buying for $10,000 in bonds. You have the choice between two bonds: A and B. The two
securities are identical except for their respective convexity. Bond B has a greater convexity than bond A.
In comparison with buying bond A, if you buy bond B:

                     a. You will lose more when interest rates increase.
                     b. You will make more when interest rates increase but loose less when interest rates
                     decrease.
                     c. You will always lose more because it means that the approximation error with
                     duration is higher.
                     d. You will always better off from movements in interest rates.




A bond has a maturity of 25 years, a coupon rate of 10% and a price of $974. Its modified duration is equal
to 5.4 years and convexity is equal to 17.5. Based on this information, what is the best estimation of the
impact on price following a 200 basis point decrease in rates?

                     a.   A decrease of $103.49
                     b.   An increase of $103.49
                     c.   A decrease of $108.60
                     d.   An increase of $108.60




It’s October 20th 2022 and you work for Teachers’ Pension Plan (Teachers). You are responsible for
evaluating the liabilities related to the Ontarian clergy’s pension plan in order to make sure the plan has the
necessary liquidity to pay out the retirement payments. After extensive analysis, the team of actuaries
working for you indicated that the pension fund will need to pay out exactly $2,500,000 on October 20th
2027 (i.e. in 5 years). This amount will serve to cover the payments to newly retired individuals. Your goal
is to make sure that the pension fund will be solvable in 5 years and that it will have the required amount
available at that time. In this context, you must immunize the pension fund’s exposure against a variation
in interest rates.
In order to construct your trading strategy, you can trade the following two bonds. The yield curve is flat at
10%. The two bonds have the following characteristics and pay annual coupons:


Bond A
Maturity: 7 years         Face Value: $1,000               Coupon Rate: 11%          YTM: 10%
Bond B
Maturity: 10 years        Face Value: $1,000               Coupon Rate: 8%           YTM: 10%


   a) What are the modified duration of these two bonds? What is the expression for the modified
      duration of a portfolio that has weights 𝑤𝐴 and 𝑤𝐵 invested in bonds A and B, respectively?
 We first need to compute the duration of each bond. We get:




  b) From your answer in a), calculate the weights you should invest in each bond in order to be
     immunized against a variation in interest rates. For each bond, clearly indicate the position
     (buy or sell) implied by the weights you found.


 We thus need to solve by setting the weights equal to the investment horizon:




Thus we buy obligation A with a weight of 1.1525% and we sell obligation B with a weight of
0.1525.


  c) What is the total amount that you need to invest today to make sure that you will have
     $2,500,000 in 5 years? How many bonds A and B will you trade today in order to achieve
     your objective? Please round the quantities to the highest integer.
We must invest at time 0:



Next we compute the price of the 2 bonds:




Now we can solve for the respective quantities:
Number of A:



Number of B:
    d) Before making the trade, the Risk Management Department of the pension fund ask you to
      check the effectiveness of your strategy in the case that interest rates would go up right after
      you trade bonds A and B. For this, the Department asks you to check the impact on your
      immunization strategy if interest rates were to increase from 10% to 11% right after you
      traded the bonds, and then stayed at 11% until October 2027. Calculate the impact that an
      increase of interest rates from 10% to 11% would have on your position in bond A and in
      bond B as of October 20th 2027 (i.e. calculate the value of the bonds and the reinvested
      coupons as of October 20th 2027). What do you conclude about the effectiveness of your
      strategy?
Revenue generated by selling A at time t=5:



Coupons reinvested from A @11% over 5 years give:




Closing our short position for B means we will need to buy it back at t=5 for a price of:




The payments of the coupon of B which we have shorted would be worth the following at t=5:



The sum of all these cash flows expressed as of time 5 is about 2.5 millions.




You are the head of the fixed income trading desk at a major financial institution. You are looking at three
governmental bonds quoted by a dealer of another bank. Each bond has a face value of $1,000, annual
payments, and the following characteristics:


        •   Bond 1: One year to maturity zero-coupon bond quoted at 𝑍𝐶1 = $970.85
        •   Bond 2: Two years to maturity zero-coupon bond quoted at 𝑍𝐶2 = $940.00
        •   Bond 3: A two years to maturity coupon bond, with 6.5% coupon rate and annual
            payments, is quoted at 𝑃𝐶 = $1,057.29.

    a) What is the one-year spot rate implied by bond 1 price?

We simply need to apply the basic discount cash flow formula of a bond:
 where z1 denotes the one year spot rate.


    b) We know that a coupon bond can be seen as portfolio of zero-coupon bonds. Bond 3 pays
       a coupon of $65 at the end of the first year and $1,065 at maturity. Replicate the two
       payments of bond 3 using bond 1 and 2. More precisely, find the quantities 𝑞1 and 𝑞2 of
       bonds 1 and 2 in order to get $65 in one year and $1,065 in two years. For this exercise,
       we assume that we can purchase fractions of bonds (no need to round 𝑞1 and 𝑞2 to the
       nearest integer). What is the cost of the replicating portfolio made of bond 1 and 2?


We know that a coupon bond can be expressed as a portfolio of zero coupons. q1=0.065 units of
ZC1 which has a face value of a 1000 gives 1000*q1 = $65 in 1 year. Similarly, q2=1.065 units
of ZC2 which has a face value of a 1000 gives 1000*q2 = $1065 in 2 years. Thus, the fair value
of the coupon paying bond should be:




    c) Is there an arbitrage opportunity? If so, explain why.


Yes there is. The coupon paying bond trade at a lower price than its replicating portfolio of zero
coupons.
    d) Describe the strategy that you would pursue in order to profit from the divergence in prices.
       For each unit of bond 3 traded, indicate the number of bond 1 and 2 that you would
       purchase or sell. What is the profit made by your strategy for each unit of bond 3 traded?

Yes there is. We should sell 0.065 units of ZC1 and 1.065 units of ZC2 which is worth 1064.21 and
buy the coupon bond which is relatively cheap to make: 1064.21 – 1057.29 = $6.92 per trade.

Question

You are head of the risk management department of a fixed income hedge fund. The market value of your
portfolio is $1,000,000, its modified duration annualized is 4, and its convexity is 200. You forecast an
increase of interest rates in the following days. As a result, you decide to immunize your exposure against
fluctuations in interest rates. To this end, you can trade a governmental bond priced at $980, with a modified
duration annualized of 8 and a convexity of 600.



    a) Based on the duration approximation of changes in price, what would be the impact of an
       increase of 2% in interest rates on the bond portfolio value?
 We simply need to apply the duration approximation formula. We get:




   b) You decide to construct a portfolio immunized against interest rate movements (i.e. Price
      Hedge). This combined portfolio should thus have a modified duration of 0 and will be
      made of the current bond portfolio plus a position in the governmental bond. What is the
      quantity of the governmental bond that needs to be purchased or sold in order to make the
      combined portfolio (i.e. current portfolio + gov. bond) immunized against interest rate?
      Please round the quantity to the highest integer.
    c) Similarly to the duration, the convexity of a portfolio is equal to the weights times the
      convexity of each constituent (i.e. 𝐶𝑋𝑃 = 𝑤𝐴 × 𝐶𝑋𝐴 + 𝑤𝐵 × 𝐶𝑋𝐵 where CX denotes the
      convexity and w denotes the weight invested). Based on this result, determine the convexity
      of the portfolio constructed in b).




which is equal to -200.
   d) Based on the duration and convexity approximation, what will be the return on the
      combined portfolio if the interest rates increase by 2%? How could we minimize this
      exposure?
